question_text: A student observes that when she increases the frequency of an electromagnetic wave, its wavelength decreases. She then asks: 'If I keep increasing the frequency indefinitely, will the wavelength approach zero?' Which statement best explains why or why not?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, wavelength will approach zero because frequency and wavelength are inversely proportional.

options.label: B
options.text: No, because the speed of light is constant, so the product of frequency and wavelength (c) cannot change. As frequency increases, wavelength must decrease, but the product always equals 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

options.label: C
options.text: No, because electromagnetic waves have a maximum frequency limit beyond which they cannot travel.

options.label: D
options.text: Yes, but only for visible light; other types of EM radiation can have wavelengths approaching zero.

explanation: The relationship between frequency and wavelength is f × λ = c (the speed of light). Since the speed of light is constant at 3 × 10⁸ m/s, wavelength can decrease but can never reach zero—as frequency increases, wavelength gets smaller proportionally to maintain the constant speed. This relationship applies to all electromagnetic waves.
